sources.notes: Statement and constraints: insert the third character between two different adjacent characters until counts of L, I, T are equal, using at most 2n operations.
Luogu solution describes the greedy simulation: repeatedly try to insert the currently least frequent character.
Codeforces community post gives a constructive target of making all three counts equal to n within exactly 2n insertions.
CSDN writeup discusses local insertion patterns for increasing chosen character counts using adjacent unequal pairs.
Maspy contest writeup was used as an additional cross-check for the constructive nature of the solution.

annotation.summary: Given a string over L, I, T, insert the missing third character between unequal adjacent characters until all three character counts are equal, or report impossible.
annotation.constraints: n <= 100 and at most 2n operations may be output, so an O(n^2) simulation is acceptable.
annotation.core_idea: If the string has no adjacent unequal pair, no operation is possible; such a string is impossible unless it was already balanced. Otherwise, one can safely aim for all three counts to become n, requiring at most 2n insertions. Repeatedly choose a currently least frequent character c. If there is an adjacent pair made of the other two characters, insert c there directly. If not, insert some other character at an available unequal pair to create a new unequal pair that allows c on the next step. This keeps raising deficient counts and the 2n budget is enough to reach the common target. A simpler implementation just repeats the least-count-first scan and stops once balanced, falling back to -1 if the 2n limit is exceeded.
annotation.complexity: O(n^2) time and O(n) memory because the string length is at most 3n during simulation
tricks: Target all three counts equal to n so the operation budget is exactly bounded by 2n.
Use an adjacent pair of the other two letters to insert a deficient letter.
If the desired pair does not exist immediately, one auxiliary insertion can create it.

solution_variants.name: least-count-greedy-simulation
solution_variants.summary: Repeatedly sort the three letters by current count and insert the first one that is possible at some adjacent unequal pair.

solution_variants.name: target-n-constructive-pattern
solution_variants.summary: Use local patterns around an unequal pair to increase selected letters until every letter appears exactly n times.

tags.evidence: The task asks for any valid sequence of operations, and the solution explicitly constructs such a sequence.
The standard simulation repeatedly inserts a character with currently minimum count whenever possible.
The construction is driven by making the three character counts equal, usually by increasing deficient letters toward a common target.
tags.description: Construct or transform an object by maintaining category counts and repeatedly fixing deficits until all required counts match.
tags.created_reason: Existing constructive and greedy tags do not capture the reusable count-equalization objective behind this solution.

tags.evidence: Operations are local insertions in a string over three symbols.
The accepted approach requires careful simulation of insertion positions and output indices.
